title: Fix broken tests — green test suite for presubmit/release gates
description:
## Goal
Get all existing tests passing reliably so they can serve as presubmit checks and release gates. Currently tests may be broken due to API changes, new features, and missing dependencies.

## Specs
- Test framework: Vitest 4.0.18 with supertest 7.2.2
- Test config: vitest.config.js (root, include: test/**/*.test.js, globals: true)
- Test scripts: npm test (vitest run), npm run test:watch, npm run test:contracts

### Test files to fix (10 total in main project):
- test/contracts/health.test.js (4 tests)
- test/contracts/sessions.test.js (8 tests)
- test/contracts/projects.test.js (11 tests)
- test/contracts/processes.test.js (5 tests)
- test/contracts/session-actions.test.js (9 tests)
- test/contracts/repos.test.js (7 tests)
- test/contracts/history.test.js (5 tests)
- test/contracts/usage.test.js (3 tests)
- test/unit/normalize-event.test.js
- test/unit/persistence-invariants.test.js

### Test helper: test/helpers/server.js
- Creates test Express app with mock dependencies
- May need updates to match new routes/v1.js endpoints (beads, chat, MCP, etc.)

### Schema validators: test/schemas/v1.js
- Validate API responses match iOS app Swift Codable structs
- May need updates for new response fields

### Also fix konnect relay tests (4 files):
- konnect/server/test/contracts/{health,auth,pairing,servers}.test.js

### Steps:
1. Run npm install to ensure dependencies are installed
2. Run npm test and capture all failures
3. Fix each failing test:
   - Update mock data to match new API shapes
   - Add missing route registrations in test helper
   - Update schema validators for new fields
   - Fix import paths if modules moved
4. Ensure all tests pass: npm test exits 0
5. Run konnect tests too: cd konnect/server && npm test

## Verification
- npm test exits with code 0, all tests pass
- cd konnect/server && npm test also passes
- No skipped tests (.skip or .todo) unless explicitly justified
- Test output shows all suites green

## Safety
- Do NOT delete failing tests — fix them
- Do NOT add .skip to make tests "pass" without fixing
- If a test is genuinely obsolete (tests removed feature), document why before removing
- Do NOT modify production code just to make tests pass — fix the tests to match current behavior

title: Map worker workspace context to bead detail view; tag sessions with bead IDs
description:
## Goal
Instead of showing dashboard cards for ephemeral worker workspaces, map that context to the bead they were assigned and show it as detail on the bead view. Tag all sessions (e.g., klaudii-iosapp:chat 1) with the beads they were involved with. Consider whether workspaces should be reused or fresh for re-worked beads.

## Specs

### 1. Bead-to-workspace association (backend):
- Currently: lib/shepherd.js (lines 239-240) derives bead ID from branch name regex: branch.match(/^bead-(.+)$/)
- This is fragile and implicit. Add explicit metadata:
  - lib/workspace-state.js: add optional 'beadId' field to workspace state
  - When shepherd dispatches (shepherd.js:271-280): set workspace state beadId = bead.id
  - GET /api/sessions (routes/v1.js:160-212): include beadId in response
  - lib/claude-chat.js: tag each chat session with associated beadId in session metadata

### 2. Session-bead tagging:
- Each chat session (workspace:sessionNum) should record which beads it worked on
- Add to claude-chat-sessions.json structure: sessions[workspace].sessions[N].beadIds = ["klaudii-abc", ...]
- When a message is sent with a bead context, append the beadId to the session's beadIds array
- API: GET /api/claude-chat/sessions/:workspace should return beadIds per session
- This creates an audit trail: "Chat 3 in workspace X worked on beads A, B, C"

### 3. Bead detail view integration (frontend):
- In the bead detail view (see: beads detail view bead):
  - Show "Worker Sessions" section listing all sessions that worked on this bead
  - Each session entry shows: workspace name, session number, status (running/completed), duration
  - If worker is currently running, show live status + stop button
  - Link to open the chat history for that session
- Hide the workspace card from the dashboard for worker-managed workspaces (see: hide worker workspaces bead)
- Instead, all worker context is visible through the bead view

### 4. Workspace reuse policy for re-worked beads:
- When a bead is reopened (status back to open after being blocked/closed):
  - Option A: Reuse existing workspace + worktree (current behavior in shepherd.js:161-173)
    - Pro: preserves context, faster startup
    - Con: stale state from previous attempt, dirty files
  - Option B: Create fresh workspace each time
    - Pro: clean slate, no stale state
    - Con: loses context, slower
  - Recommended: Fresh workspace by default, but tag both old and new sessions with the bead
  - The bead detail view shows ALL sessions across all attempts, providing full history
- When reusing: shepherd should git reset --hard and clean before re-dispatching

### 5. Backend data model:
- Extend workspace-state.json:
  ```
  workspace: { mode, sessions, drafts, beadId?, type: "worker"|"user" }
  ```
- Extend claude-chat-sessions.json:
  ```
  workspace: { current, sessions: { "N": { cliSessionId, beadIds: [...] } } }
  ```
- New API: GET /api/beads/:id/sessions — returns all sessions that worked on this bead

## Verification
- Shepherd dispatches bead → workspace state includes beadId
- Chat sessions record beadId in their metadata
- GET /api/beads/:id/sessions returns all worker sessions for that bead
- Bead detail view shows worker session history
- Re-worked bead shows sessions from both attempts
- Worker workspace cards hidden from dashboard (visible through bead view)

## Safety
- Do NOT delete worker session history when bead is closed
- Do NOT break existing session tracking for user workspaces
- Preserve backward compatibility with sessions that have no beadIds
- Worker workspace cleanup should respect running processes
- Depends on: beads detail view, hide worker workspaces

title: Add UI/E2E testing with Playwright
description:
## Goal
Add browser-based UI testing to prevent UI features from breaking each other. Without automated UI tests, CSS changes, JS refactors, and new features will constantly cause regressions in the dashboard and chat views.

## Specs
- Current state: no UI testing exists. @vitest/browser-playwright appears in package-lock.json but is not an active dependency.
- Framework choice: Playwright (modern, fast, reliable, good Vitest integration)

### Setup:
1. Install Playwright: npm install -D @playwright/test
2. Install browsers: npx playwright install chromium
3. Create playwright.config.js at repo root
4. Create test/e2e/ directory for UI tests
5. Add npm script: "test:e2e": "npx playwright test"
6. Configure Playwright to:
   - Start the local server before tests (webServer config)
   - Use a test-specific port and config
   - Run headless by default (headed for debugging)

### Test scenarios to cover:
1. Dashboard rendering:
   - Page loads, workspace cards appear
   - Theme toggle works (light/dark mode switch)
   - Beads section visible and populated
2. Workspace card interactions:
   - Run mode pill cycles through modes on click
   - Running status pill displays correctly
   - Stop/restart buttons function
3. Chat view:
   - Opening a workspace chat loads the chat panel
   - Sending a message renders user bubble
   - Tool pills render correctly
   - Session dropdown appears with multiple sessions
4. Beads panel:
   - Filter buttons work (All/Open/In Progress/Blocked/Closed)
   - Creating a new bead via form works
   - Bead detail view opens on click (once implemented)
5. New workspace modal:
   - Modal opens on button click
   - Form validation works
   - Workspace creation succeeds

### Test data:
- Use mock/demo mode or seed test data before each test
- Existing test helper creates mock sessions — may need to extend for UI tests
- Tests should be self-contained (setup → action → assert → cleanup)

## Verification
- npm run test:e2e exits 0 with all tests passing
- Tests run in CI (headless Chromium)
- Tests cover at least dashboard, chat, and beads panel
- Screenshot comparison available for visual regression (optional first pass)
- Tests complete in under 60 seconds

## Safety
- Do NOT use real user data in E2E tests
- Do NOT leave browser processes running after tests
- Playwright webServer should use a dedicated test port (not 9876)
- Tests should not depend on external services (Claude API, GitHub, etc.)
- Keep test suite fast — use parallel test execution where possible

title: Add functional/integration test suite
description:
## Goal
Add functional (integration) tests that verify end-to-end workflows beyond the existing contract tests. These should test real user scenarios like creating a workspace, sending a chat message, and getting a response.

## Specs
- Current testing: contract tests validate API response shapes with mocked dependencies
- Missing: tests that exercise actual business logic flows with real (or near-real) dependencies

### Test scenarios to cover:
1. Workspace lifecycle: create workspace → verify state → start session → stop → remove
2. Chat flow: send message → relay spawns → events stream → history persists
3. Beads CRUD: create bead → update status → add comment → close → verify in list
4. Session management: create chat 1 → create chat 2 → switch → verify history isolation
5. Workspace state persistence: set mode → restart server → verify mode persisted
6. Settings CRUD (when settings bead is implemented): save → load → verify
7. Relay lifecycle: spawn → crash → reconnect → verify recovery

### Implementation:
- Create test/functional/ directory
- Use Vitest with supertest (same as existing tests)
- Tests should use real server.js (not the mock helper) with test-specific config
- Isolate test data: use temp directories for workspace state, conversations, etc.
- Clean up after each test (rm temp dirs)
- Add npm script: "test:functional": "vitest run test/functional/"

### Test helper needs:
- Test server factory that starts real Express app with real routes
- Temp directory management for isolated state
- Utilities to wait for async operations (relay spawn, etc.)

## Verification
- npm run test:functional exits 0
- Tests cover at least the 7 scenarios listed above
- Tests are isolated and don't affect real user data
- Tests clean up temp files/processes after running
- Can run in CI without special environment

## Safety
- Do NOT use real user workspaces or data for testing
- Do NOT leave orphaned processes (tmux, relay daemons) after tests
- Temp directories must be cleaned up even on test failure (use afterEach/afterAll)
- Do NOT test against production/real Claude API — mock the Claude CLI interaction

title: Beads panel: first-class detail view with comments
description:
## Goal
Make beads clickable to open a full detail view (like a real issue tracker), with the ability to view and add comments, see full description with markdown rendering, dependencies, history, and associated workspace/session info.

## Specs
- Current state: public/app.js lines 1375-1403 renders bead rows with expandable inline description
- Current state: PATCH /api/beads/:id in routes/v1.js (line 730) already supports comment field
- Current state: GET /api/beads/:id (line 696) returns individual bead details
- Current state: bd CLI supports comments via 'bd comment' and 'bd show' returns comments

### Detail View UI:
- When user clicks a bead row, open a detail panel (could be a slide-out panel from the right, or a modal)
- Detail view should show:
  1. Full bead ID and title
  2. Status badge (editable — dropdown to change status)
  3. Priority badge (editable)
  4. Assignee (editable)
  5. Full description with markdown rendering (use existing markdown renderer if available)
  6. Dependencies section (blocks/blocked-by/related)
  7. Comments section — chronological list of all comments
  8. "Add comment" text input + submit button
  9. Timestamps (created, updated)
  10. Associated workspace/session info (if any — link to worker workspace)
  11. Event history/audit trail

### Backend changes:
- GET /api/beads/:id needs to return comments array
  - bd show --json already includes comments if available
  - May need: bd comments list <id> --json for full comment list
- POST /api/beads/:id/comment endpoint for adding comments
  - Or continue using PATCH /api/beads/:id with comment field

### Frontend changes:
- public/app.js: add openBeadDetail(beadId) function
- Add detail panel HTML to public/index.html (or dynamically create)
- public/style.css: add styles for detail panel, comments list, comment form
- Wire bead row click to open detail view (currently clicks toggleBeadDesc)

## Verification
- Click any bead in the list → detail panel opens showing full info
- Can add a comment via the detail panel → comment appears in the list
- Can change status/priority/assignee from detail panel
- Close detail panel → return to list view
- Refresh page → reopen detail, comments persist
- bd show <id> --json from CLI shows comments added via UI

## Safety
- Do NOT remove the list view — detail is an additional view, not a replacement
- Do NOT break existing bead CRUD API
- Sanitize comment content to prevent XSS
- Preserve existing bd CLI comment compatibility

title: Chat UI: Consolidate consecutive tool pills into grouped summary
description:
## Goal
When multiple tool uses of the same type happen back-to-back (e.g., 4 consecutive Read calls, or 3 Bash commands), consolidate them into a single grouped pill like "Read 4 files" or "Ran 3 commands" with a combined expandable details section. Also show a consolidated status line like "Ran 3 commands, Read 4 files, Ran 1 command, thinking..." for the overall activity.

## Specs
- File: public/gemini.js
  - geminiAppendToolUse() (lines 1426-1457): currently creates individual pill per tool_use
  - handleGeminiEvent() case "tool_use" (lines 553-587): dispatches each tool_use individually
  - geminiRenderCompletedTool() (lines 1369-1419): renders individual completed tools from history
  - geminiShowChat() (lines 2103-2152): history rendering pipeline, also individual per tool

- Implementation approach:
  1. Add a batching/accumulator layer before pill rendering:
     - When a tool_use event arrives, check if the previous rendered element is a tool pill of the same tool type
     - If yes, merge into a grouped pill instead of creating a new one
     - Group pill summary shows: "{ToolName} ({count})" e.g., "Read (4 files)" or "Bash (3 commands)"
  2. Grouped pill details section:
     - Show each individual tool call as a sub-section within the combined details
     - Each sub-section should show: file path or command, parameters, and result
     - Use collapsible sub-sections for each individual call
  3. For the streaming/running state:
     - Show "Reading file 3 of 4..." or "Running command 2 of 3..." as the group updates
     - When all complete, show final summary like "Read 4 files (all succeeded)"
  4. For history rendering (geminiShowChat and geminiRenderRecoveredContent):
     - Group consecutive tool_use+tool_result pairs of the same tool type
     - Render as grouped pill with all sub-results

- CSS: public/gemini.css lines 306-444
  - Add styles for grouped pill header, sub-sections, counters
  - Distinguish grouped pills visually (maybe slightly different background or icon)

- Edge cases:
  - Mixed results (3 success, 1 error) → show group as partial: "Read 4 files (1 error)"
  - Interrupted group (tool_use of different type in between) → close current group, start new
  - Single tool use → render as before (no grouping needed)

## Verification
- Send a message that triggers 4 Read calls → see "Read (4 files)" single pill with expandable sub-sections
- Send a message that triggers 3 Bash + 2 Read + 1 Bash → see "Ran 3 commands", "Read 2 files", "Ran 1 command"
- Verify individual results still visible by expanding the grouped pill
- History reload renders same grouped format
- Single tool uses still render as individual pills

## Safety
- Do NOT break the tool_id matching between tool_use and tool_result
- Do NOT remove the ability to see individual tool parameters and results
- Preserve existing special handling for Edit (diff view), AskUserQuestion, ExitPlanMode
- Do NOT group different tool types together (Read + Bash = separate groups)

title: Dashboard cards: show managed freerange Claudes with stop controls
description:
## Goal
Freerange Claude instances managed by Klaudii should be visible on their workspace cards as running, with options to stop individual instances.

## Specs
- Currently, workspace cards show a single running/stopped status based on tmux session state
- File: public/app.js, renderSessions() lines 130-269
  - Line 257: status badge shows "running" if relayActive even if tmux shows "stopped"
  - Line 204-210: running panel shows single Open/Stop/Restart/Terminal/History buttons
- File: routes/v1.js, GET /api/sessions (lines 160-212):
  - Returns chatActive (streaming), relayActive (relay alive), status (tmux state)
  - Need to also return: list of active relay PIDs/session IDs per workspace
- File: lib/claude-chat.js:
  - activeRelays Map (line 325) tracks one relay per workspace
  - isActive() (lines 1014-1016) checks if workspace has an active relay
  - Need: expose all active relays per workspace, not just boolean
- Backend changes:
  - Extend GET /api/sessions to include relay/process details: array of { sessionId, pid, startedAt }
  - Add API to stop individual relay: POST /api/workspace/:workspace/relay/:sessionId/stop
- Frontend changes:
  - Show a list of running Claude instances on the card (not just one "running" badge)
  - Each instance should have its own stop button
  - Show session number or identifier for each instance

## Verification
- Start 2 Claude sessions in the same workspace
- Dashboard card shows both as running with individual stop buttons
- Stopping one does not affect the other
- Card updates in real-time when a Claude instance exits

## Safety
- Do NOT auto-kill existing relays when showing the list
- Ensure stop only kills the targeted relay, not all relays for the workspace
- Preserve backward compatibility with single-relay workspaces

title: Architect/Shepherd dedicated chat with role context and memory injection
description:
## Goal
Allow users to open a dedicated chat to the Architect or Shepherd agent at any time, with the agent receiving full role context and recent memories on startup.

## Specs
- Frontend (public/app.js or public/index.html):
  - Add UI entry points to start an architect or shepherd chat:
    - Option A: Dedicated buttons in dashboard header or sidebar (e.g. "Talk to Architect", "Talk to Shepherd")
    - Option B: Special workspace type selector when creating a new workspace
  - When opened, the chat UI (public/gemini.js) should show the agent role label (architect/shepherd) prominently
- Backend (server.js / routes/v1.js / lib/claude-chat.js):
  - When starting an architect chat:
    - Load ARCHITECT_STARTUP.md as the initial system context
    - Load planning/architect-system.md as additional context
    - Inject last 50 memories from GET /api/memory/architect (depends on memory database bead)
    - Include instruction: "You have access to your memory journal. Store important decisions and observations before ending this session."
  - When starting a shepherd chat:
    - Load lib/shepherd-prompt.md as the initial system context
    - Inject last 50 memories from GET /api/memory/shepherd
    - Same memory instruction
  - Add MCP tools for memory read/write so the agent can access memories during conversation
- Memory lifecycle:
  - On session start: inject memories as context
  - During session: agent can store new memories via MCP tool or API
  - On session end (if agent chooses to "land the plane"): prompt to store final observations
  - Agent can request to load more memories if needed (e.g. "load 100 more")

## Verification
- User can open an architect chat from the dashboard
- Architect sees its role context (ARCHITECT_STARTUP.md content) and recent memories
- User can open a shepherd chat from the dashboard
- Shepherd sees its role context and recent memories
- Both agents can store and retrieve memories during conversation

## Safety
- Do NOT auto-start architect/shepherd — user must explicitly open the chat
- Do NOT modify existing workspace chat behavior
- Memory injection should be clearly labeled in the chat context so agents know it's historical
- Depends on: Architect/Shepherd persistent memory database (SQLite)

title: Architect/Shepherd persistent memory database (SQLite)
description:
## Goal
Create a persistent memory/journal system for the Architect and Shepherd agents so they can maintain long-term context across sessions, simulating a very long-running conversation.

## Specs
- Location: ~/Library/Application Support/com.klaudii.server/memory.sqlite
- Schema:
  - Table: memories
    - id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT
    - agent TEXT NOT NULL ("architect" | "shepherd")
    - workspace TEXT (nullable, for workspace-specific memories)
    - content TEXT NOT NULL (the memory/journal entry)
    - category TEXT (e.g. "decision", "observation", "todo", "context")
    - created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
    - session_id TEXT (to group memories by session)
  - Table: memory_metadata
    - key TEXT PRIMARY KEY
    - value TEXT
    - (stores things like last_session_id, total_memories, etc.)
- API endpoints (routes/v1.js):
  - GET /api/memory/:agent — returns last N memories (default 50, configurable via ?limit=)
  - POST /api/memory/:agent — store a new memory { content, category?, workspace? }
  - GET /api/memory/:agent/search?q= — full-text search memories
- Library: use better-sqlite3 (already common in Node ecosystem, synchronous, fast)
- Add better-sqlite3 to package.json dependencies
- Create lib/memory.js for database initialization, CRUD operations, and search
- Memory injection: when starting an architect/shepherd session, inject the last 50 memories as context
- Memory save: agents should be instructed (via prompt) to store important observations, decisions, and context as memories before shutdown

## Verification
- POST /api/memory/architect with { content: "Test memory" } returns 200
- GET /api/memory/architect returns the stored memory
- GET /api/memory/architect?limit=5 returns at most 5 memories
- SQLite file exists at ~/Library/Application Support/com.klaudii.server/memory.sqlite
- npm test passes

## Safety
- Do NOT store secrets or credentials in memories
- Use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
- Handle missing/corrupt database gracefully (auto-recreate)
- Do NOT modify existing chat history storage

title: Add user settings panel (gear icon near Kloud Konnect)
description:
## Goal
Add a settings/preferences UI accessible via a gear icon in the header, near the Kloud Konnect button. This is the first user-configurable settings system for Klaudii.

## Specs
- File: public/index.html — header section (around line 40)
- Current header: Klaudii title | status badge | auth status | Kloud Konnect button | theme toggle
- Add a gear icon button between the Kloud Konnect button and the theme toggle
- Settings panel should be a modal (similar to the existing cloud modal pattern at openCloudModal())
- Initial settings to support:
  1. Worker workspace visibility: "hide" / "show" / "auto-clean" (default: "hide")
  2. Theme preference (could move theme toggle here too, but keep the quick-toggle in header)
- Settings persistence:
  - Store in ~/Library/Application Support/com.klaudii.server/settings.json
  - Add GET/PATCH /api/settings endpoints in routes/v1.js
  - Load settings on app startup and expose to frontend
- CSS: style the gear button to match existing .btn-theme style (public/style.css lines 700-710)
- Add settings modal HTML structure to index.html
- Frontend JS: add openSettingsModal(), saveSettings() functions to public/app.js

## Verification
- Gear icon visible in header next to Kloud Konnect button
- Clicking opens a modal with configurable settings
- Changing a setting persists across page refresh
- GET /api/settings returns current settings JSON
- PATCH /api/settings updates and persists settings

## Safety
- Do NOT move existing functionality into settings without keeping quick-access available
- Settings file should have sensible defaults if missing/corrupt
- Do NOT store secrets or credentials in settings.json

title: Hide or auto-cleanup worker agent workspaces on dashboard
description:
## Goal
Worker agent workspaces (created by the shepherd for task execution) should be hidden from the dashboard by default, or auto-cleaned up after completion. Behavior should be user-configurable via a new settings UI.

## Specs
- Worker workspaces are created by lib/shepherd.js (lines 148-214) when dispatching beads to workers
- They appear in GET /api/sessions (routes/v1.js:160-212) alongside user-created workspaces
- Workers operate in git worktrees created via tmux sessions
- Need a way to tag workspaces as "worker-managed" vs "user-created":
  - Add a field to workspace state (lib/workspace-state.js) like { type: "worker" | "user" }
  - Shepherd sets type: "worker" when creating workspaces
  - User-created workspaces default to type: "user"
- Dashboard filtering (public/app.js, renderSessions() lines 130-269):
  - By default, hide workspaces where type === "worker"
  - Add a toggle/filter to show/hide worker workspaces
- Auto-cleanup options (configurable):
  - "hide" — keep but don't show on dashboard (default)
  - "auto-clean" — remove workspace after bead is closed and git worktree is merged/clean
  - "show" — show all workspaces like today
- This depends on the settings UI bead (see: "Add user settings panel")

## Verification
- Create a worker workspace via shepherd dispatch
- Verify it does NOT appear on dashboard by default
- Toggle "show worker workspaces" — it appears
- Set auto-clean mode — workspace removed after task completion
- User-created workspaces are never auto-cleaned

## Safety
- Do NOT delete worker workspaces that have uncommitted/unpushed changes
- Do NOT delete workspaces with running Claude instances
- Always check git status before auto-cleanup

title: Dashboard: Run mode pill click not registering reliably
description:
## Goal
Fix the finicky click behavior on the chat-mode pill in the dashboard card view so run mode cycling (gemini → claude-local → claude-remote) registers every click.

## Specs
- File: public/app.js, function cycleChatMode() (lines 57-76)
- The pill is rendered inside the card at line 237-239, with onclick="cycleChatMode(event, '...')"
- The click target is small (pill with padding 2px 6px, font-size 10px) — likely the click is hitting the card underneath or the mode-dot span instead of the pill
- CSS for pill: public/style.css lines 395-430, class .chat-mode-pill
- The event.stopPropagation() is already called but the issue may be:
  1. The .mode-dot span (5px circle) inside the pill eating the click
  2. The pill click area being too small on mobile/trackpad
  3. A race between the pill innerHTML replacement and the next click
- Fix: increase the click target size (min-height, padding), ensure pointer-events on child elements pass through to parent, and/or debounce the innerHTML update

## Verification
- Click the run mode pill 10 times rapidly — every click should cycle the mode
- Verify PATCH to /api/workspace-state/:workspace fires on each click
- Test on both desktop and mobile viewports

## Safety
- Do NOT change the cycling order or mode labels
- Do NOT modify the PATCH API call behavior

title: Klaudii task scheduler: periodic task execution as a service feature
description:
Goal: Add a general-purpose task scheduler to Klaudii so periodic tasks (Shepherd, build sheriff, health checks, etc.) are a first-class feature — configurable, observable, and manageable from the dashboard.

Specs:
- Create lib/scheduler.js:
  - Manages named scheduled tasks with configurable intervals
  - Each task has: name, interval (ms), enabled flag, handler function, last run time, last result
  - API: scheduler.register(name, interval, handler), scheduler.start(), scheduler.stop(), scheduler.pause(name), scheduler.resume(name)
  - Runs handlers on setInterval, wraps each in try/catch
  - Logs run summaries to server console
- Register the Shepherd as the first scheduled task (default: every 5 minutes)
- Add REST endpoints:
  - GET /api/scheduler — list all scheduled tasks with status
  - POST /api/scheduler/:name/pause — pause a task
  - POST /api/scheduler/:name/resume — resume a task
  - POST /api/scheduler/:name/trigger — run a task immediately
- Wire into server.js startup

Future tasks that would use this: build sheriff, worktree cleanup, bead sync, health checks.

Verification:
- Server starts without errors
- GET /api/scheduler shows the shepherd task registered and running
- POST /api/scheduler/shepherd/trigger runs the shepherd immediately
- POST /api/scheduler/shepherd/pause pauses it, /resume resumes it
- Server logs show shepherd runs on schedule

Safety:
- Do NOT break existing server startup
- Errors in one scheduled task must not affect others
- Each run must be wrapped in try/catch with logging
- Test that the server starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple

title: Move Shepherd from cron to server-integrated timer
description:
Goal: Move the Shepherd from an external cron job into a timer inside the Klaudii server process so it starts automatically and has direct access to internal state.

Specs:
- Create lib/shepherd-scheduler.js that:
  - Exports a start(deps) function called from server.js
  - Runs lib/shepherd.js logic on a setInterval (default 5 minutes, configurable)
  - Has direct access to server internals (projects, tmux, workspaceState, claudeChat, etc.) via injected deps — no HTTP round-trips to itself
  - Logs each run summary to the server console
  - Exposes a way to pause/resume (for future dashboard control)
- Modify server.js to import and start the scheduler after server initialization
- The shepherd.js core logic should be refactored to accept deps directly instead of making curl/fetch calls to localhost
- Keep bin/shepherd.sh as a manual trigger option but it's no longer the primary way to run
- The --claude mode (spawning a Claude for complex decisions) should still work

Verification:
- Server starts without errors: node server.js
- Server logs show shepherd runs every 5 minutes
- With a ready bead, the shepherd dispatches a worker automatically
- Stopping the server stops the shepherd (no orphan cron jobs)

Safety:
- Do NOT break existing server startup — shepherd is additive
- The scheduler must handle errors gracefully (one failed run doesn't crash the server)
- Add a try/catch around each shepherd run
- Test that the server starts and the first shepherd run completes before closing

CHALLENGE:simple

title: Shepherd Claude: periodic monitoring script
description:
Goal: Create a shepherd script that boots, reads system state, takes action, and exits. Designed to run every ~5 minutes via cron or a Klaudii scheduler.

Specs:
- Create file: lib/shepherd.js — the core logic
- Create file: bin/shepherd.sh — entry point that runs: CLAUDECODE="" claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions < lib/shepherd-prompt.md
- Create file: lib/shepherd-prompt.md — the prompt given to the Shepherd Claude each run

The shepherd-prompt.md should instruct the Shepherd Claude to:
1. Read all beads: curl http://localhost:9876/api/beads
2. Check workspace status: curl http://localhost:9876/api/sessions
3. For each in-progress workspace:
   a. Is the worker still running? (check session status)
   b. Has it been stuck too long? (check lastActivity timestamp, >15 min = stuck)
   c. Any bead comments with questions?
4. For ready/open beads with no assignee:
   a. If a workspace is available, create one and start a worker
   b. Worker launch command: tmux new-session + CLAUDECODE="" claude --dangerously-skip-permissions
   c. Send the standard worker prompt template with the bead ID
5. For stuck workspaces:
   a. Check git status in the worktree
   b. If significant uncommitted work, commit it as WIP and mark bead blocked
   c. If no progress, kill the session and mark bead for retry
6. Print a summary of actions taken to stdout
7. Exit

The shepherd uses the REST API (curl) not MCP — it's a simple script, not a long-running MCP client.

bin/shepherd.sh should be runnable via cron:
*/5 * * * * /Volumes/Fast/bryantinsley/repos/klaudii/bin/shepherd.sh >> /tmp/shepherd.log 2>&1

Verification:
- bin/shepherd.sh runs and exits cleanly when there are no ready beads
- With a ready bead, it creates a workspace and starts a worker
- With a stuck workspace (>15 min no activity), it takes action
- Output is a readable summary of what it did

Safety:
- Do NOT kill workspaces that are actively working (check lastActivity)
- Do NOT create more than 3 concurrent workspaces (configurable limit)
- The shepherd must be idempotent — running it twice in a row should be safe
- Do NOT modify beads directly — use the REST API endpoints
- Test by running bin/shepherd.sh manually before closing

CHALLENGE:complex

title: Integrate MCP server into Klaudii service via SSE transport
description:
Goal: Move the MCP server from a standalone stdio process into the Klaudii Express server, exposed as an SSE endpoint so all Claude Code instances connect to one shared MCP server.

Specs:
- Add MCP SSE endpoint to server.js (or a new route file)
- Use @modelcontextprotocol/sdk with SSE transport (not stdio)
- Mount at /mcp or similar path on the existing Express server (:9876)
- Migrate all 7 tools from mcp/klaudii-mcp-server.js:
  1. klaudii_list_workspaces — uses existing GET /api/sessions logic directly (no HTTP round-trip)
  2. klaudii_create_workspace — uses existing POST /api/sessions/new logic directly
  3. klaudii_send_message — uses existing chat send logic directly
  4. klaudii_get_status — uses existing workspace state logic directly
  5. klaudii_read_beads — reads .beads/issues.jsonl or shells out to bd
  6. klaudii_create_bead — shells out to bd create
  7. klaudii_update_bead — shells out to bd update
- Since the MCP server IS the Klaudii server, tools can call internal functions directly instead of making HTTP requests to themselves
- Update mcp/README.md with new Claude Code config:
  {
    "mcpServers": {
      "klaudii": {
        "type": "sse",
        "url": "http://localhost:9876/mcp"
      }
    }
  }
- The old mcp/klaudii-mcp-server.js can remain as a fallback but is no longer the primary

Verification:
- npm install in project root picks up @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
- node server.js starts without errors
- Connecting to http://localhost:9876/mcp via SSE shows MCP handshake
- A Claude Code instance configured with the SSE URL can list workspaces and read beads
- Existing REST API and WebSocket functionality still works

Safety:
- Do NOT break existing server.js functionality — MCP is additive
- Do NOT remove existing REST endpoints — they're still used by the dashboard
- If @modelcontextprotocol/sdk needs to be added to the main package.json, do so
- Test that the server starts and existing endpoints respond before closing

CHALLENGE:complex

title: Klaudii MCP server
description:
Goal: Create a Node.js MCP server that wraps the Klaudii REST API, allowing Claude Code CLI instances (the Architect) to manage workspaces and beads via native tool calls.

Specs:
- Create file: mcp/klaudii-mcp-server.js
- Use @modelcontextprotocol/sdk package (install as dependency)
- Implement these MCP tools:
  1. klaudii_list_workspaces() — GET /api/sessions, return simplified workspace list
  2. klaudii_create_workspace(repo, branch, bead_id?) — POST /api/sessions/new
  3. klaudii_send_message(workspace, message, sender?) — POST /api/chat/:workspace/send
  4. klaudii_get_status(workspace) — GET /api/chat/:workspace/status
  5. klaudii_read_beads(status?, priority?) — GET /api/beads with optional filters
  6. klaudii_create_bead(title, description, priority?, type?, deps?) — POST /api/beads
  7. klaudii_update_bead(id, status?, comment?, assignee?) — PATCH /api/beads/:id
- Server communicates via stdio (standard MCP transport)
- Base URL configurable via KLAUDII_URL env var (default http://localhost:9876)
- Create mcp/package.json with the @modelcontextprotocol/sdk dependency
- Add a README.md in mcp/ with Claude Code MCP config example:
  {
    "mcpServers": {
      "klaudii": {
        "command": "node",
        "args": ["<path>/mcp/klaudii-mcp-server.js"]
      }
    }
  }

Verification:
- cd mcp && npm install succeeds
- node mcp/klaudii-mcp-server.js starts without errors (will wait for stdio input)
- With Klaudii server running, the MCP tools return expected data
- Add the MCP config to a Claude Code instance and verify tools appear

Safety:
- Do NOT modify any existing Klaudii files — this is a new standalone module
- Do NOT bundle or inline the MCP SDK — use npm dependency
- Do NOT hardcode any API keys or secrets
- Keep it simple: plain HTTP fetch to localhost, no auth needed
- Run npm install and verify the server starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple

title: Message sender field + colored chat bubbles
description:
Goal: Add a sender field to chat messages so the dashboard can render Architect and Shepherd messages with distinct bubble colors.

Specs:
- In lib/claude-chat.js pushHistory(): accept optional sender parameter (default "user" for role=user, "assistant" for role=assistant)
- In lib/gemini.js equivalent history functions: same sender parameter
- Persist sender in the history JSON: each message object gets a "sender" field
  Valid values: "user", "assistant", "architect", "shepherd"
- In server.js WebSocket handler: when broadcasting user_message events, include sender field
- In public/gemini.js (frontend): when rendering chat messages, check the sender field
  - "user" → existing user bubble style
  - "assistant" → existing assistant bubble style
  - "architect" → dark green background (#166534), white text
  - "shepherd" → amber/dark orange background (#92400e), white text
- Add CSS classes: .bubble-architect, .bubble-shepherd in the chat CSS
- Backwards compatible: messages without sender field render as before

Verification:
- Server starts without errors: node server.js
- Open dashboard, send a message in a workspace chat — renders as normal user bubble
- Manually POST to /api/chat/:workspace/send with sender:"architect" — message renders with dark green bubble
- Existing chat history (no sender field) still renders correctly

Safety:
- Do NOT break existing chat rendering — sender field is additive
- Do NOT change the WebSocket message protocol shape — only add optional sender field
- Test with an existing workspace that has chat history to verify backwards compat
- Run the server and verify it starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple

title: REST API: Beads CRUD endpoints
description:
Goal: Add REST endpoints that expose Beads operations so the MCP server (and any HTTP client) can read, create, and update Beads without shelling out to bd.

Specs:
- Add routes in routes/v1.js:
  - GET /api/beads — returns all beads as JSON array (parse .beads/issues.jsonl)
  - GET /api/beads/:id — returns single bead by ID
  - POST /api/beads — create a new bead. Body: { title, description, priority?, type?, deps? }
    Shells out to: bd create "title" --description="desc" -p N -t type --deps ... --json
  - PATCH /api/beads/:id — update bead. Body: { status?, comment?, assignee?, priority? }
    Shells out to: bd update <id> --status X --json (and/or bd comment <id> "text")
- All endpoints return JSON
- Use child_process.execSync for bd commands (synchronous is fine for this)
- Set cwd to the main repo path (config.reposDir + repo name, or a new config field)
- Handle bd command failures gracefully (return 500 with error message)

Verification:
- Server starts without errors: node server.js
- GET /api/beads returns JSON array of existing beads
- POST /api/beads with {title:"test",description:"test bead"} creates a bead and returns it
- PATCH /api/beads/:id with {status:"in_progress"} updates the bead
- GET /api/beads/:id returns the updated bead

Safety:
- Do NOT modify .beads/ files directly — always go through bd CLI
- Do NOT install any new npm packages — use built-in child_process
- Sanitize the bead ID parameter to prevent command injection (alphanumeric + hyphens only)
- Run the server and verify it starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple
close_reason: Done — GET /api/beads, GET /api/beads/:id, POST /api/beads, PATCH /api/beads/:id all implemented with bd CLI, input sanitization, and JSON responses

title: REST API: GET /api/chat/:workspace/status
description:
Goal: Add a REST endpoint that returns the status of a workspace's chat session, including whether a Claude is active, streaming state, and any pending questions from Bead comments.

Specs:
- Add route in routes/v1.js: GET /api/chat/:workspace/status
- Response JSON:
  {
    workspace: string,
    relayActive: boolean,       // claudeChat.isActive(workspace)
    streaming: boolean,         // workspaceState.isStreaming(workspace)
    chatMode: string,           // workspaceState mode
    lastActivity: number,       // timestamp
    pendingPermission: object|null  // any pending approval request
  }
- Return 404 if workspace/project not found

Verification:
- Server starts without errors: node server.js
- GET /api/chat/some-workspace/status returns 200 with expected shape
- Fields match the actual workspace state

Safety:
- Do NOT modify existing routes — only add new one
- This is a read-only endpoint, no state mutations
- Run the server and verify it starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple
close_reason: Done — GET /api/chat/:workspace/status returns relayActive, streaming, chatMode, lastActivity, pendingPermission

title: REST API: POST /api/chat/:workspace/send
description:
Goal: Add a REST endpoint that sends a message to a workspace Claude session, so the Architect and Shepherd can communicate with Workers without using WebSocket.

Specs:
- Add route in routes/v1.js: POST /api/chat/:workspace/send
- Accept JSON body: { message: string, sender?: "user"|"architect"|"shepherd" }
- Default sender to "user" if not provided
- For workspaces with active Claude relay: use claudeChat.appendMessage()
- For workspaces without active relay: use claudeChat.sendMessage() to start new conversation
- Persist the sender field alongside chat history messages
- Return { ok: true, workspace } on success
- Return 404 if workspace/project not found
- Return 400 if message is empty

Verification:
- Server starts without errors: node server.js
- With a running workspace, POST to /api/chat/:workspace/send with {"message":"hello","sender":"architect"} returns 200
- The message appears in the workspace chat history with sender field preserved
- Existing WebSocket chat functionality is unaffected

Safety:
- Do NOT modify the WebSocket handler logic — only add the REST route
- Do NOT change the claudeChat module's public API — only use existing methods
- If claudeChat needs changes for sender tracking, make them additive only
- Run the server and verify it starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple
close_reason: Done — POST /api/chat/:workspace/send implemented in routes/v1.js with sender field support in pushHistory

title: Beads list panel: compact row redesign with short IDs
description: ## Goal Redesign the beads list rows to be more compact: remove the Close/Block action buttons from the list view (those belong in the detail view), show a truncated title, and use just the last 3 alphanumeric characters after the dash as the visual ID (e.g., *2d6* instead of klaudii-2d6). ## Specs - File: public/app.js, renderBead() function (lines 1375-1403) - Current row: [full ID] [title] [status] [priority] [assignee] [updated] [Block btn] [Close btn] ### Changes: 1. Remove action buttons (Block/Close) from the list row: - Delete lines 1385-1390 that generate actionBtns HTML - These actions move to the detail view (see: beads detail view bead) 2. Short ID format: - Current: .bead-id shows full "klaudii-yha" (70px min-width, 10px monospace) - New: extract last 3 chars after the dash, display as bold/italic short code - Example: "klaudii-yha" → "*yha*", "klaudii-2d6" → "*2d6*" - Render as: yha - CSS: slightly bolder, use accent color or italic to distinguish from title - Full ID shown on hover via title attribute 3. Truncated title: - Current: full title text with click-to-expand description - New: truncate with CSS text-overflow: ellipsis (max ~60 chars visible) - Still clickable — but now opens detail view instead of inline expand - Remove inline description expansion (toggleBeadDesc) in favor of detail view 4. Revised row layout: [*yha*] [Truncated Title...] [status pill] [P1] - Remove assignee and updated from the compact row (visible in detail view) - Make the row more scannable ### CSS changes (public/style.css): - .bead-short-id: font-weight: 600, font-family: monospace, color: var(--text-secondary) - .bead-title: text-overflow: ellipsis, white-space: nowrap, overflow: hidden - .bead-row: reduce padding, tighten layout - Remove .bead-actions styles (or keep for detail view) ## Verification - Bead list shows compact rows: short ID, truncated title, status, priority - No Block/Close buttons visible in the list - Hovering over short ID shows full "klaudii-yha" in tooltip - Clicking a row opens the detail view (not inline expand) - List is scannable and compact with many beads ## Safety - Do NOT remove the action functionality — just move it to detail view - Keep full ID accessible (hover tooltip, detail view) - This bead depends on the detail view bead being implemented first or concurrently

title: Chat session dropdown visibility inconsistent
description:
## Goal
Fix the chat session dropdown (for switching between Chat 1, Chat 2, etc.) which intermittently appears and disappears. The "New" chat button works reliably, but the dropdown to switch between existing chats is inconsistent.

## Specs
- File: public/index.html, line 118:
  <select id="gemini-session" class="gemini-session-select hidden" ...>
- File: public/gemini.js, geminiFetchSessions() (lines 98-128):
  - Fetches sessions from /api/claude-chat/sessions/:workspace or /api/gemini/sessions/:workspace
  - Toggles visibility: select.classList.toggle("hidden", sessions.length < 1)
  - Issue: condition is sessions.length < 1, but should probably be sessions.length < 2
    (no point showing a dropdown with only 1 option)
  - The dropdown may be hidden when it should be visible due to:
    1. Race condition: geminiFetchSessions() called before sessions API returns correctly
    2. The session count check using < 1 instead of <= 1
    3. The dropdown getting re-hidden on workspace switch or panel toggle
- File: public/gemini.js, geminiShowChat() calls geminiFetchSessions() — check if this is called consistently
- Investigate: is the dropdown rebuilt on every poll/refresh? Could it flicker due to async timing?

## Verification
- Create Chat 1 in workspace — dropdown hidden (only 1 session, nothing to switch to)
- Create Chat 2 — dropdown appears and stays visible
- Switch to Chat 1 via dropdown — dropdown remains visible
- Navigate away and back — dropdown still shows both chats
- Refresh page — dropdown correctly shows after reload

## Safety
- Do NOT change session creation/deletion behavior
- Do NOT auto-switch sessions when dropdown appears
- Preserve the "New" chat button functionality

title: Dashboard cards: multiple stop buttons for multiple relays per workspace
description:
## Goal
When multiple chats exist in the same workspace (each spawning its own relay), the dashboard card needs to show stop buttons for each running relay, not just a single stop button.

## Specs
- Context: Starting chat 1 in a workspace spawns relay 1. Starting chat 2 spawns relay 2. Both run concurrently. This is correct behavior — we should NOT implicitly kill chat 1 when chat 2 starts.
- File: lib/claude-chat.js
  - Relay lifecycle: sendMessage() (lines 866-938) spawns a relay daemon per session
  - activeRelays Map tracks relays per workspace
  - Currently: one relay per workspace in the Map
  - Need: Map value should be an array/map of { sessionNum → relayInfo }
  - Or track per-session relays separately
- File: public/app.js, renderSessions() lines 130-269
  - Line 207: single Stop button calls stopSession(project)
  - Need: enumerate all active relays for the workspace and render a stop button per relay
  - Show as "Stop Chat 1", "Stop Chat 2", etc.
  - Or a compact list: "2 chats running [Stop All] [Stop 1] [Stop 2]"
- File: routes/v1.js
  - GET /api/sessions should return session-level relay status
  - Add relay count and per-session status to the response
- Related to: "Dashboard cards: show managed freerange Claudes with stop controls" — these can share the same backend infrastructure

## Verification
- Open workspace, start Chat 1 (relay 1 starts)
- Create Chat 2 in same workspace (relay 2 starts)
- Dashboard card shows both relays with individual stop buttons
- Stop Chat 1 — only Chat 1's relay dies, Chat 2 continues
- Card updates to show 1 relay remaining

## Safety
- Do NOT change relay spawning behavior — each chat should still get its own relay
- Do NOT auto-kill other relays when stopping one
- Backward compatible with workspaces that only have 1 chat/relay

title: Address test-results.md findings — triage and create beads
description:
## Goal
Review the test-results.md file in the repo root and create individual beads for each actionable bug or feature request found in the test results. Then clean up or archive the test-results.md file.

## Specs
- File: test-results.md (repo root)
- Contains 11 test cases from 2026-03-05 with PASS/FAIL/PARTIAL results
- Known issues found in the test results (create separate beads for each):
  1. AskUserQuestion tool result pill appears separately instead of being absorbed into card UI
  2. No cost/token footer in chat — need cumulative conversation counter in chat input area
  3. Extended thinking not implemented — needs toggle control
  4. Long-running tool pills need client-side elapsed time counter
  5. Message ordering issues after interrupt (stop/interrupt test)
  6. Stop button stuck visible when idle
  7. Draft sync leaking across workspaces (no workspace isolation)
  8. Tool pills don't finalize in originating window (multi-window)
  9. Mystery blue blinking dot — should be removed
  10. Stale draft state on page load
- After creating individual beads for each finding:
  - Move test-results.md to planning/test-results-2026-03-05.md (archive it)
  - Or delete it if all findings are captured in beads
- Each sub-bead should reference this parent bead with discovered-from dependency

## Verification
- Every actionable item in test-results.md has a corresponding bead
- test-results.md is no longer in the repo root (moved to planning/ or deleted)
- bd list shows all new beads
- git status is clean

## Safety
- Do NOT close beads for items that aren't actually fixed
- Read the full test-results.md before creating beads — don't miss items
- Preserve test result data in the bead descriptions for context

title: Light mode backgrounds too stark/white — darken to #DDDDDD minimum
description:
## Goal
Darken the light mode background colors so the theme is less eye-searingly white. All primary backgrounds should be #DDDDDD or darker.

## Specs
- File: public/style.css, lines 47-79, html.light { ... } block
- Also: public/wireframe-chat.html lines 45-75 has a DUPLICATE set of light mode vars (must update both)
- Current values that are too light (all above #DDDDDD):
  - --bg: #edf0f5 (237,240,245) → needs darkening, e.g. #d0d4dc
  - --bg-card: #f5f7fa (245,247,250) → needs darkening, e.g. #dde0e6
  - --bg-input: #f5f7fa → same as card, darken similarly
  - --bg-btn: #eef0f5 → darken, e.g. #d5d8e0
  - --bg-btn-hover: #e2e5ec → already close, still lighten slightly less
  - --bg-hover: #e4e7ec → acceptable but consider darkening
  - --bg-badge: #e5e8f0 → acceptable but consider
  - --bg-active: #dde2ed → already near threshold
- Approach: shift the entire light palette down ~15-20% luminance while maintaining the blue-gray hue
- Text colors (--text, --text-strong, etc.) should remain dark and may need slight adjustment for contrast
- Border colors may need adjustment for visibility against darker backgrounds

## Verification
- Toggle to light mode: all backgrounds should feel "medium-light" not "white"
- No background color variable in html.light should be lighter than #DDDDDD
- Text must remain clearly readable (WCAG AA contrast)
- Cards should still be distinguishable from the page background
- Test both dashboard and chat views

## Safety
- Do NOT change dark mode colors
- Do NOT change semantic colors (--s-green, --s-red, etc.) unless readability requires it
- Keep both style.css and wireframe-chat.html in sync

title: Dashboard: Running pill too light green, hard to read
description:
## Goal
Darken the running status pill on dashboard workspace cards so the white text is legible against the green background.

## Specs
- File: public/style.css, lines 387-391, class .card-status.running
- Current: background: linear-gradient(to right, #86efac, #4ade80, #22c55e); color: #fff
- The lightest color #86efac is too pale for white text readability
- Also check: .card-accent.running uses var(--s-green) = #4ade80 (line 316)
- Fix: darken the gradient stops. Suggested: linear-gradient(to right, #22c55e, #16a34a, #15803d)
  - These are Tailwind green-500, green-600, green-700
- Ensure WCAG AA contrast ratio (4.5:1 minimum) for white text on the darkest and lightest gradient stops

## Verification
- Running pill text "running" is clearly readable against the green background
- Check contrast ratio: use Chrome DevTools color picker or online contrast checker
- Both dark mode and light mode should look good (light mode --s-green is #15803d)

## Safety
- Only change the gradient color stops, not the pill shape, font-size, or padding
- Do NOT change the light-mode semantic green (--s-green) as it's used elsewhere

title: Dashboard: Beads task tracker panel with CRUD
description:
Goal: Add a Beads (task tracker) section to the Klaudii dashboard that shows all beads with filtering, and allows creating new beads and updating their status.

Specs:
- Add a new collapsible section to public/index.html titled Beads after the Scheduler section
- Show beads in a table/list with columns: ID, Title, Status, Priority, Assignee, Updated
- Status badges with colors: open=blue, in_progress=yellow, blocked=red, closed=green
- Filter tabs: All, Open, In Progress, Blocked, Closed
- Create New Bead button that opens a form with: title, description (textarea), priority (0-4 dropdown), type (task/bug/feature/epic)
- Each bead row has: a clickable title that expands to show full description, and quick-action buttons (Close, Block)
- Fetch data from GET /api/beads
- Create via POST /api/beads with JSON body
- Update via PATCH /api/beads/:id with JSON body
- Auto-refresh every 30 seconds (share timer with scheduler section)
- Match existing dashboard styling
- Add the JS to public/app.js in a clearly delineated section

Verification:
- Open http://localhost:9876 -- Beads section is visible
- Shows existing beads (all 11 currently closed)
- Click filter tabs to filter by status
- Click Create New Bead, fill form, submit -- new bead appears
- Click a bead title to expand description
- Close/Block buttons update bead status
- Works in both dark and light theme

Safety:
- Do NOT modify any existing sections or cards
- Only ADD new code -- beads section, its CSS, and its JS
- Do NOT touch server.js or any backend files
- Do NOT modify the /api/beads endpoints

title: Dashboard: Scheduler panel with pause/resume/trigger controls
description:
Goal: Add a scheduler section to the Klaudii dashboard that shows all registered scheduled tasks with their status, and provides controls to pause, resume, and manually trigger each task.

Specs:
- Add a new collapsible section to public/index.html titled Scheduler between the existing sections
- The section should list each scheduled task as a card with:
  - Task name
  - Interval (human-readable, e.g. every 5 min)
  - Status: enabled/disabled badge, running indicator
  - Last run time (relative, e.g. 2 min ago)
  - Last result or error
- Action buttons per task: Pause/Resume toggle, Trigger Now
- Fetch data from GET /api/scheduler
- Pause via POST /api/scheduler/:name/pause
- Resume via POST /api/scheduler/:name/resume
- Trigger via POST /api/scheduler/:name/trigger
- Auto-refresh every 30 seconds
- Match existing dashboard styling (CSS custom properties, dark/light theme)
- Add the JS to public/app.js in a clearly delineated section

Verification:
- Open http://localhost:9876 -- scheduler section is visible
- Shows shepherd task with every 5 min, enabled status
- Click Trigger Now -- task runs, lastRunAt updates
- Click Pause -- badge shows disabled, click Resume -- back to enabled
- Works in both dark and light theme

Safety:
- Do NOT modify any existing sections or cards
- Only ADD new code -- scheduler section, its CSS, and its JS
- Do NOT touch server.js or any backend files

title: Clean worktree guarantee for worker sessions
description:
Goal: Ensure every worker workspace starts with a clean git worktree, preventing stale beads changes or uncommitted code from previous sessions from confusing the next worker.

Specs:
- In routes/v1.js POST /api/sessions/new: after git.addWorktree(), verify clean state
- In routes/v1.js POST /api/sessions/start (restart existing): add a pre-start cleanup step
  - git reset --hard
  - git clean -fd
  - git fetch origin
  - git checkout -B <branch> origin/main (or origin/<base-branch>)
- Add a helper function cleanWorktree(worktreePath, baseBranch) in lib/git.js
- The helper should be idempotent and safe to call on already-clean worktrees
- Log the cleanup steps for debugging

Verification:
- Create a workspace, make some uncommitted changes in its worktree
- Restart the session via POST /api/sessions/start
- Verify the worktree is clean: git status shows no changes
- New workspaces via POST /api/sessions/new still work as before

Safety:
- Do NOT modify existing worktree creation logic — only add cleanup before session start
- Do NOT delete worktrees — only reset their state
- The cleanup MUST NOT run on the main repo, only on worktree paths
- Add a safety check: refuse to clean if path is the main repo root
- Run the server and verify it starts before closing

CHALLENGE:simple

title: Trunk-based dev: presubmit tests + CI + build sheriff
description:
Goal: Establish a trunk-based development workflow where all agents commit directly to main, with fast presubmit tests gating commits and expensive tests running in GitHub Actions CI.

Specs:
Phase A — Presubmit (local, fast):
- Add a pre-push git hook (or pre-commit) that runs fast tests before allowing push to main
- Fast tests = linting, syntax checks, unit tests if any exist
- Must complete in <30 seconds to avoid blocking agents
- Hook should be installed by bd init or a setup script

Phase B — CI (GitHub Actions):
- Add a GitHub Actions workflow on push to main
- Runs the full test suite (integration tests, slower checks)
- Reports pass/fail status
- Keeps a record of which commit broke what

Phase C — Build Sheriff:
- A Claude that runs whenever CI reports a failure on main
- Reads the CI failure log
- Identifies the commit that broke it (git bisect or just the latest push)
- Either: files a fix Bead, or reverts the offending commit
- This is a specialized Shepherd variant — ephemeral, triggered by CI failure

Design notes:
- All workers commit directly to main (trunk-based)
- Worktrees use throwaway branches only to satisfy git worktree constraints, then push to main
- No long-lived feature branches
- Small, verified commits (2-10 min tasks) make reverts cheap
- The build sheriff is the safety net that makes this sustainable

Verification:
- Pre-push hook blocks a push with failing tests
- CI workflow runs on push to main and reports status
- Build sheriff responds to CI failures (can be tested with an intentional break)

Safety:
- Pre-push hook must have a bypass flag for emergencies (--no-verify, documented)
- Build sheriff must NOT force-push or rewrite history — only revert commits or file fix Beads
- CI workflow must not expose secrets in logs

CHALLENGE:complex
